scientificCalculators.title: Experimental Uncertainty Calculator
scientificCalculators.subtitle: Statistical Analysis of Laboratory Measurements
scientificCalculators.description: Add measurement values to calculate statistical parameters including mean, absolute deviation, standard deviation, and measurement uncertainty, following Physical Chemistry laboratory methodology.

table.emptyHint: Add at least 2 values to calculate uncertainty

probableMean.label: Arithmetic Mean (x̄)
probableMean.tooltip: Most probable value: sum of all measurements divided by number of measurements

meanAbsoluteDeviation.label: Mean Absolute Deviation (Δx)
meanAbsoluteDeviation.tooltip: Average of absolute deviations from the mean - represents measurement uncertainty

standardDeviation.label: Sample Standard Deviation (s)
standardDeviation.tooltip: Square root of variance - measure of data dispersion

relativeUncertainty.label: Relative Uncertainty (εᵣ)
relativeUncertainty.tooltip: Mean absolute deviation expressed as percentage of the mean

finalResult.label: Measured Value with Uncertainty
finalResult.tooltip: Best estimate of true value: mean ± uncertainty

uncertainty.title: What is Measurement Uncertainty?
uncertainty.content: Measurement uncertainty quantifies the dispersion of values that could reasonably be attributed to a measured quantity. It is impossible to find the exact value of a quantity, as it can only be measured with an instrument that always has limitations. The true value is unknown, so we represent the measurement by a confidence interval.
uncertainty.example: Example: Millimeter ruler (smallest division = 1 mm) → precision ≈ 0.5 mm. Reading: 7.8 cm → result: 7.80 ± 0.05 cm

meanDeviation.title: Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)
meanDeviation.content: The mean absolute deviation measures the average distance between each data point and the mean. It provides a robust measure of variability, less sensitive to outliers than standard deviation. It is often used to represent measurement uncertainty (Δx).

standardDeviation.title: Standard Deviation (σ or s)
standardDeviation.content: Standard deviation is the most important measure of variation, as it considers all sample values. For experimental analysis, we use sample standard deviation (s) when working with a sample of the population. The standard deviation of the mean (σx̄ = s/√n) is used to express final uncertainty.

significantFigures.title: Significant Figures
significantFigures.content: These are all digits obtained in the measurement process. Zeros included to locate the decimal point are not significant (leading zeros). Uncertainty should contain only ONE significant figure, and the mean should be rounded to the same decimal place as the uncertainty.
significantFigures.formula: Rules: 1) Non-zero digits are always significant. 2) Zeros between non-zero digits are significant. 3) Leading zeros are not significant. 4) Trailing zeros in a number with a decimal point are significant.

rounding.title: Rounding Criteria
rounding.content: Rounding should only be done at the end of all calculations, as each rounding introduces error that can accumulate. If the digit to the right of the rounding point is 0-4: discard the right part. If it is 5-9: increment the left digit.
rounding.example: Measurement: (0.126446 ± 0.0005885) V → Adjusting uncertainty to 1 SF: (0.1264 ± 0.0006) V

messages.minValues: Add at least 2 values to calculate uncertainties
